key_departures.impact: Led team with 85 catches, 1,013 yards, 4 TDs in 2025 — the first Patriots WR to eclipse 1,000 yards since 2019. Released for cap reasons ($26.5M cap hit in 2026) and age/legal concerns before an additional $6M became guaranteed. Leaves a massive void as the clear WR1.
Ranked first on the team in 2025 with 73 pressures and 10.5 sacks. Signed a 1-year, $12M deal with Washington. Top pass rusher on the roster walks out the door, escalating EDGE need.

Signed a 3-year, $21M deal with the Chiefs. Tonga was the Patriots' top run-stuffing DT and showed surprising pass-rush capability (best pressure grade of career in 2025). Departure hurts interior D-line depth and run defense.

Traded to Chicago for a 2027 5th-round pick. Started all 17 regular-season games and all 4 playoff games in 2025. Departure precipitated the need to shift Jared Wilson from LG to C and acquire a new left guard, creating the IOL void Vera-Tucker was signed to fill.
Signed a 1-year, $3.25M deal with Atlanta. Was the Patriots' TE2 behind Hunter Henry. Departure creates a need for a second tight end, though Julian Hill signing partially addresses this.

Signed a 2-year deal with Baltimore. Was a starting safety for the Patriots in 2025, recording 4 interceptions (matching career total). Departure opened the starting safety spot that Byard was signed to fill.

Signed a 2-year, $12M deal with the 49ers. Served as the swing tackle who made 25 starts during his time in New England, stepping in admirably when Will Campbell was injured in 2025 (Week 12). Departure leaves the team without a proven swing tackle backup.
Released with a $4.86M cap charge. Was one of the stoutest edge setters in football and racked up 217 career tackles and 7.5 sacks over five seasons. Loses a key run-defense edge anchor.
Released. Was a special teams stalwart and rotational LB for the Patriots. Depth loss on special teams.
Signed a 1-year deal with Miami. Was an RFA who the Patriots did not tender. Depth loss at CB but not a starter role.

signings.analysis: 4-year/$68M deal (up to $80M with incentives) with $39M guaranteed — a legitimate investment in the WR1 role vacated by Diggs. Doubs, 25, had 55 catches, 724 yards, and 6 TDs in 2025 as Green Bay's top receiver. Contract structure (2-year out at $35M guaranteed with low 2026 cap hit of $8.6M) is well-structured, but Doubs has never topped 724 yards in a season and hasn't proven to be a true elite WR1. He addresses the need partially: WR upgrades from critical (Tier 1) to moderate (Tier 2), but the team almost certainly still wants another wide receiver in the draft or via trade (A.J. Brown discussions ongoing). Mack Hollins, Kayshon Boutte, Kyle Williams, and Efton Chism III round out a thin room.

3-year/$42M deal ($21M fully guaranteed) at LG, directly filling the hole created by the Bradbury trade (which opened up Jared Wilson to move to center). AVT was graded PFF's 10th-best guard in 2024 (4th-lowest pressure rate allowed at 2.8%), but missed the entire 2025 season with a torn triceps — his third serious injury in five years. Contract is intelligently structured with $4.25M per-game roster bonuses annually that only kick in when he's active, limiting downside. With $21M in guarantees on a 3-year deal, this is real starter money, but the injury risk means the IOL need isn't fully erased — team could still address interior depth/center depth in the draft. Need drops from Tier 1 (post-Bradbury trade) to Tier 2.

3-year/$36.5M deal with $23.1M guaranteed (guaranteed in years 1 and 2 effectively). Jones had a career-high 7 sacks and 51 pressures in 2025 across the Titans and Ravens. Brings positional versatility — can play edge or kick inside to 3-tech. However, at an AAV of $12.17M, this is mid-tier edge money (34th-highest-paid edge per Pats Pulpit) — not the elite pass rusher upgrade the team needs after losing Chaisson and Jennings. Jones replaces Chaisson's production in theory, but the Patriots were 26th in sacks and 26th in pressures in 2025, and Jones alone doesn't solve the chronic pass rush problem. Harold Landry (under contract through 2027) remains the projected top edge. EDGE still a meaningful need — drops from Tier 1 (critical) to Tier 2 (still need investment).

1-year/$9M deal with no guaranteed money per OverTheCap. Byard led the NFL with 7 interceptions in 2025, earned 1st-team All-Pro honors for a third time, and has never missed a game in 10 years (164 consecutive regular-season games). Reuniting with Vrabel from their Titans days. He's the ideal starter next to 2025 4th-round pick Craig Woodson. Despite his elite production, the 1-year structure signals a bridge — need is addressed for 2026 but the team will need to find a long-term answer at the second safety spot, likely via the 2026 or 2027 draft. Need drops from Tier 2 to Tier 3 for 2026.

3-year/$12M deal ($6M fully guaranteed) — a fullback signing, not a traditional RB. Gilliam spent 6 seasons with the Bills (94 games). Josh McDaniels has long employed a fullback in his system. This is a scheme-fit signing that signals the Patriots intend to run a more physical, FB-heavy offense. Not filling a pre-existing positional need but a schematic addition. Will work alongside Rhamondre Stevenson and TreVeyon Henderson in the backfield.

3-year/$18M (up to, per ESPN; Spotrac has $18M AAV of $6M) with ~$6M guaranteed. Hill is a 6'4", 251 lb blocking tight end who played 45 games (26 starts) over three years in Miami, valued primarily for his toughness and blocking ability (68.4% of career snaps as a blocker). He fills the TE2/blocking role vacated by Austin Hooper's departure to Atlanta. However, with just 33 career receptions, 0 TDs, and minimal pass-catching ability, Hill does not address New England's longer-term need for a receiving TE2 behind Hunter Henry (who is in the final year of his contract at age 31). TE remains a draft priority — likely Rounds 2-4.

1-year deal. Britt was a special teams standout with the Dolphins in 2025 (77% of ST snaps). Slots in behind starters Robert Spillane and Christian Elliss, partially replacing the special teams roles vacated by released Tavai and Jennings. Depth-level signing — LB need unchanged at starter level.

1-year veteran minimum-level deal. Brown played under Vrabel with the Titans in 2023 (37% defensive snap share in 2024). Limited to 9 games in 2025. Projects as depth/special teams behind Byard and Craig Woodson. Minimal impact on S need which is already addressed at the starter level by Byard.

Veteran minimum deal. Luketa is primarily a special teams contributor (546 career ST snaps vs 367 defensive snaps). Did not play in 2025 after being on the Rams practice squad. A camp body / depth piece, not a starter solution. EDGE need unchanged at the starter level.

Re-signed RFA on a 2-year/$4.4M deal ($2M guaranteed). DeVito was the emergency QB3 behind Drake Maye and Joshua Dobbs in 2025 and did not take a snap. This is a cheap re-sign for practice squad value and emergency depth. QB remains a non-need with Maye as the franchise QB and Dobbs on the final year of his deal.

recommended_need_adjustments.reasoning: Stefon Diggs' release (1,013 yards in 2025) created a Tier 1 crisis that the Doubs signing partially resolves. Doubs (4yr/$68M, $39M gtd) is a legitimate WR1 investment for a 25-year-old, but he has never topped 724 receiving yards in a season and is not a proven No. 1 receiver. The room behind him (Hollins, Boutte, Kyle Williams, Chism) is thin. WR remains a Tier 2 need — high draft priority (Rounds 1-3) and an A.J. Brown trade is reportedly still being discussed. Downgrade from acute Tier 1 crisis to ongoing Tier 2 need.

The team lost both Chaisson (10.5 sacks, team's top pass rusher) and Jennings (key run-defense edge) while adding only Jones ($12.2M AAV, mid-tier) and minimum-deal Luketa. Jones replaces starter production but doesn't improve the unit. Harold Landry (under contract) and Jones form a functional but unspectacular duo — team was 26th in sacks and pressures in 2025. EDGE remains a draft priority, likely Rounds 1-3. Drops from Tier 1 (critical) to Tier 2 (important) — not solved, just stabilized.

Bradbury trade created an immediate LG void, which the Vera-Tucker signing (3yr/$42M, $21M gtd) directly addresses. If healthy, AVT is a top-10 guard. However, his injury history (3 season-ending injuries in 5 years, missed all of 2025) introduces real risk. Jared Wilson moving to center is a projection, not a proven solution. IOL drops from Tier 1 (post-trade emergency) to Tier 2 — needs draft capital at C depth and IOL insurance.

Khyiris Tonga (3yr/$21M to Chiefs) was the team's top run-stuffing DT and showed surprising pass-rush upside. No replacement has been signed at DT — Christian Barmore (charges dropped), Cory Durden, and developmental players are the current depth. This is an unchanged need that may have escalated slightly. IDL remains a Tier 2 need for the draft.

Jaylinn Hawkins' departure (2yr deal with Ravens) created a starting void, but Kevin Byard (1yr/$9M, 3x All-Pro, NFL-leading 7 INTs in 2025) directly fills it next to Craig Woodson. Byard is elite production but on a 1-year deal, meaning the long-term question remains open. For 2026, S drops to Tier 3 — addressed with a starter-caliber player. Team should add a developmental safety in Rounds 4-6 for future planning.

Austin Hooper's departure to the Falcons and Hunter Henry being 31 and in the final year of his contract escalate TE need. Julian Hill (3yr/$18M) fills the TE2 blocking role but is not a receiving threat (33 career receptions). The team almost certainly needs a receiving TE via the draft — likely Rounds 2-4 in an extraordinarily deep TE class this year. TE escalates from Tier 3 to Tier 2.

Vederian Lowe's departure to San Francisco (2yr/$12M) leaves the team without a proven swing tackle behind Will Campbell (LT) and Morgan Moses (RT). GM Eliot Wolf specifically called OT behind those two a remaining area of need. No swing tackle was added in free agency. OT depth escalates from Tier 3 to Tier 2.

Alex Austin did not receive an RFA tender and signed with Miami, but he was a depth player. Christian Gonzalez remains the clear starter. No significant CB signing or loss. CB remains Tier 3 — depth is a valid concern for the draft but not urgent.

key_additions.headline: 25-year-old WR1 investment (4yr/$68M) replaces Diggs with multi-year commitment; led Packers in receptions and yards in 2025
Former 1st-round LG steps in after Bradbury trade; PFF's 10th-best guard in 2024 but carries significant injury risk
Career-high 7 sacks/51 pressures in 2025; mid-tier edge upgrade replacing departing Chaisson with 2-year guaranteed commitment
3x All-Pro, NFL's 2025 INT leader (7) reunites with Vrabel; fills Hawkins' starting spot next to Craig Woodson
McDaniels-scheme fullback signals commitment to power run game alongside Stevenson and Henderson
Blocking TE2 replaces Hooper in the 12-personnel sets; limited receiving profile means passing TE still needed in draft

key_losses.headline: Team's WR1 (85 rec, 1,013 yds, 4 TD in 2025) released for cap reasons; Doubs signing is partial replacement
Team's top pass rusher (10.5 sacks, 73 pressures) walks to NFC East rival; Dre'Mont Jones is the in-house replacement

key_losses.contract_summary: Traded to Chicago Bears for 2027 5th-round pick
key_losses.headline: Starting center (17 regular season + 4 playoff starts) traded away; Jared Wilson slides to C, Vera-Tucker fills LG
Top DT run-stuffer and surprise pass rusher earns big payday from Chiefs; interior D-line now thin
Starting safety (4 INTs in 2025) departs; Kevin Byard signed as the direct replacement
Swing tackle who made 25 career starts (including starting for injured Campbell in 2025) departs; OT depth now a concern
